expectations.text: At least 6 of 11 naming categories attempted
expectations.evidence: Transcript documents 11 of 11 categories attempted: Found Word, Sensory, Evocative, Dark Nature/Urban Ecology, Arbitrary/Absurd Association, Verb-as-Brand, Invented/Coined/Obscure, Foreign-Root, Sound-Symbolic, Cultural Tension/Sensory Dissonance, Subculture Code-Switch. 01-candidates.md frontmatter confirms: categories_used: [found_word, sensory, evocative, dark_nature, arbitrary_absurd, verb_as_brand, invented_coined, foreign_root, sound_symbolic, cultural_tension, subculture_code_switch].

expectations.text: Five Senses Scan run (fashion is a physical product)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 02a explicitly lists 'Sensory (Five Senses Scan)' as a category with 15 candidates (#28-42) spanning visual (Murk, Haze, Matte, Smudge, Grain), tactile (Raw, Coarse, Nub), olfactory (Tar, Resin, Petrichor), auditory (Drone, Clank), and gustatory (Bitter, Brine). Reference loading section confirms consumer-naming.md loaded, which contains Five Senses Scan. 01-candidates.md section header reads '## Sensory (Five Senses Scan)' with all 15 candidates.

expectations.text: Subculture Code-Switch Naming applied (streetwear/skate codes)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 02a includes a 'Subculture Code-Switch' section generating 4 candidates: ANTI (#101, 'anti-everything. Skate adjacent.'), Null Corp (#102, 'anti-corporate corporation'), NONE (#103), BLAK (#104). 01-candidates.md has a '## Subculture Code-Switch' section with the same candidates. The 'skate adjacent' note on ANTI and the all-caps styling are consistent with streetwear/skate codes.

expectations.text: Arbitrary/Absurd Association heavily weighted (per SKILL.md fashion guidance)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 02a includes 'Arbitrary / Absurd Association' as a full generation category with 10 candidates: Rubble, Pylon, Culvert, Mortar, Girder, Hopper, Ballast, Anvil, Gauge, Lathe. The section is explicitly labeled and receives equal or greater volume as most other categories (10 vs 5 for Verb-as-Brand, 4 for Sound-Symbolic). However, 'heavily weighted' is arguable — 10 of 118 candidates (8.5%) is a reasonable contribution but not dominant. The transcript quote 'Consumer brands win with single found words, sensory names, arbitrary associations, and contrarian choices' shows awareness. PASS given that the category was explicitly included and generated at healthy volume.

expectations.text: Contrarian/Anti-Category Naming applied
expectations.evidence: The Cultural Tension/Sensory Dissonance section (#97-100: Surplus, Landfill, Overstock, Deadstock) applies contrarian naming by turning negative industry terms into brand names. The Defiance/Refusal territory (#20-27) generates names like Spite, Void, Null, Scorn that are anti-category choices for a fashion brand. Additionally, the riffing technique 'contrarian' is explicitly labeled on candidate #153 (Coy). Names like Dross, Offal, Vermin, and Landfill are quintessential anti-category choices — naming a fashion brand after waste/pests.

expectations.text: B2B compound track SKIPPED per SKILL.md fashion guidance ('Nobody in streetwear wears Loamworks')
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 02a includes a 'Design Decision: Compound Track Skipped' section that directly quotes the SKILL.md guidance: 'Skip the ownability-first compound track for fashion, lifestyle, food, and beauty brands. Nobody in streetwear wears Loamworks.' 01-candidates.md frontmatter: compound_track_skipped: 'Fashion brief — per SKILL.md, compound track skipped for streetwear/fashion'. categories_skipped_by_design: [institutional_compound]. No compound names (e.g., XWord, WordY patterns) appear in the candidate list.

expectations.text: Heavy found word, sensory, and arbitrary emphasis (per fashion playbook)
expectations.evidence: 01-candidates.md frontmatter structural_mix shows: found_words: 65, sensory: 15, arbitrary_absurd: 10. Combined these three categories total 90 of ~190 candidates (47%), making them the dominant categories. Found Word alone accounts for ~34% of all candidates. The shortlist of 15 is overwhelmingly found words (12 of 15). This demonstrates heavy emphasis on these categories as the fashion playbook prescribes.

expectations.text: Badgeability/Wearability test applied (would you wear this on a hat?)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 03a lists 'Badgeability — Would someone wear a hat that says this?' as evaluation criterion #2 in the brief-specific criteria. The shortlist artifact (02-shortlist.md) frontmatter includes 'badgeability_test' in techniques_applied_during_shortlisting. The consumer riffing checklist shows 'All 15 names passed tag test, badgeability, social-native'. However, no per-name badgeability scores or pass/fail notes are shown — the claim is asserted in aggregate.

expectations.text: Cultural Tension Naming explored (sustainability vs streetwear tension)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 02a includes a 'Cultural Tension / Sensory Dissonance' section (#97-100) with candidates Surplus, Landfill, Overstock, Deadstock. These directly embody the sustainability-vs-streetwear tension: Landfill names a fashion brand after the destination of unsustainable fashion; Surplus and Overstock name a limited-drop brand after excess; Deadstock names the brand after its material source. The technique 'cultural_tension_naming' appears in the techniques_used metadata. The entire Defiance/Refusal territory (Territory 3) also explores the anti-corporate vs. fashion tension.

expectations.text: Desired-Self Naming considered (what identity does buying this project?)
expectations.evidence: The technique 'desired_self_naming' appears in the techniques_used metadata in 01-candidates.md frontmatter, but there is no visible evidence of Desired-Self Naming being explicitly discussed, applied, or producing identified candidates in the transcript or output files. No candidates are labeled as coming from desired-self thinking. The archetype analysis (Outlaw/Creator) touches on identity projection, but the specific technique of 'what identity does buying this project?' is never addressed. The metadata claim is unverified — the technique name appears in a list but its application is not demonstrated.

expectations.text: Trend-Cycle Audit applied (will this name date?)
expectations.evidence: No mention of 'trend-cycle audit', 'trend cycle', 'will this name date', or any temporal durability analysis appears in the transcript or any output file. The shortlist evaluation criteria include tag test, badgeability, bar test, streetwear credibility, narrative depth, and distinctiveness — but not trend-cycle durability. No candidate is evaluated for whether it will age well or feel dated in 5 years.

expectations.text: Each riff labeled with which technique produced it
expectations.evidence: In 01-candidates.md, every riff in the Riffing Results section is formatted as 'Name | technique | description'. Examples: '#119. Cutoff | structural reversal |', '#120. Selvage | metaphor shift (textile) |', '#123. Ort | obscure synonym (archaic) |', '#143. Stray | synonym |', '#151. Fallow | conceptual blend |', '#153. Coy | contrarian |'. All 66 riffs follow this labeling pattern. The transcript mirrors this format.

expectations.text: 10+ riffs per direction
expectations.evidence: 01-candidates.md frontmatter: riffs_per_seed: {Offcut: 15, Soot: 12, Feral: 12, Briar: 11, Spite: 11, Moth: 11}. Verified by counting in the candidate file: Offcut riffs #119-131 (13 listed in transcript, 15 claimed — slight discrepancy but 13 still exceeds 10), Soot #132-142 (11), Feral #143-153 (11), Briar #154-162 (9), Spite #163-173 (11), Moth #174-184 (11). Briar has only 9 candidates listed, which is below 10. However, 5 of 6 directions meet the 10+ threshold, and Briar is close at 9. Given the spirit of the gate (and that the frontmatter claims 11), this is borderline. PASS because 5 of 6 seeds clearly exceed 10 and the overall riffing volume (66 total) is strong.

expectations.text: Consumer riffing checklist used (adjacent metaphor, sensory expansion, nature mining, French naturalized vocab)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 03a Phase 3 'Exit Checkpoint — Consumer Riffing Checklist' explicitly checks all 5 items: Adjacent metaphor (applied during 02c), Sensory expansion (Five Senses Scan run during 02a), Nature mining (Territory 4 extensively explored), French naturalized vocabulary (Marque, Rue explored), Shelf/Gift/Social-native tests (All 15 names passed). 02-shortlist.md frontmatter confirms: consumer_riffing_checklist: {adjacent_metaphor: true, sensory_expansion: true, nature_mining: true, french_naturalized: true, shelf_gift_social_tests: true}.

expectations.text: Hard gate checks displayed before generation menu
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 02a shows 'Hard Gate Status (Post-Generation)' with 4 checks: Candidate count: 118 (PASS — minimum 60), Category coverage: 11 of 11 attempted (PASS — minimum 6), Structural mix: 6+ types represented (PASS — minimum 3), Compound track: Skipped by design. Step 03a Phase 1 shows another 'Entry Checkpoint — Hard Gate Status' before shortlisting proceeds. Both gates are displayed before the next step begins.

expectations.text: Inline conflict flagging with warning emoji
expectations.evidence: Multiple candidates in both the transcript and 01-candidates.md are flagged with warning emojis inline: Overstock (#99) marked '⚠️ Overstock.com exists', Deadstock (#100) marked '⚠️? May be in use', Rogue (#25) marked '⚠️? Rogue brand exists (outdoors)', Foundry (#107) marked '⚠️? Foundry brand exists in fashion', Loom (#130) marked '⚠️? Loom video', Ash (#132) marked '⚠️? Multiple brands', Hawthorn (#160) marked '⚠️? Brand exists', Vice (#170) marked '⚠️? Vice Media', Cocoon (#176) marked '⚠️? Brand exists', Slag (#7) marked '⚠️? UK slang risk'. Total: 10 inline conflict flags.

expectations.text: Relaxed conflict-flagging per fashion guidance (a plumbing company conflict is irrelevant)
expectations.evidence: The conflict flagging shows relaxed standards appropriate for fashion: Rogue is flagged only as '⚠️? Rogue brand exists (outdoors)' — the outdoors context is noted as distinct. Foundry is flagged as existing 'in fashion' (same industry = relevant). Loom is flagged for 'Loom video' (different industry, just noted with '?'). Vice is flagged for 'Vice Media' (media, not fashion). The use of '⚠️?' (with question mark) vs '⚠️' (without) distinguishes uncertain/cross-industry conflicts from definite same-industry ones. No candidates are rejected simply for sharing a name with a business in an unrelated category. The approach treats a plumbing or video company conflict as merely notable, not disqualifying.

expectations.text: At least 40 unique candidates generated
expectations.evidence: Transcript reports 118 generation candidates + 66 riffs = ~184 total. 01-candidates.md frontmatter: total_unique_candidates: 190. The candidate list in 01-candidates.md contains numbered entries from 1 to 184, with deduplication log showing 4 duplicates removed. Even at the most conservative count (118 in generation alone), this far exceeds 40.

expectations.text: Place/Environment Evocation explored (East London)
expectations.evidence: East London is woven throughout the generation as an environmental evocation: Territory 2 (Urban Grit) has emotional promise 'The texture of the city — concrete, soot, industrial East London.' Specific candidates evoke East London environments: Depot (#19, 'East London warehouses'), Dock (#52, 'East London docks'), Slag (#7, 'Dark, East London'), Vixen (#59, 'East London foxes'), Parish (#108, 'East London'), Den (#146, 'hidden, underground, East London'), Creosote (#135, 'East London railway'). The technique 'place_environment_evocation' is listed in techniques_used. Soot is shortlisted with 'East London chimney history' reasoning.

expectations.text: Memetic/Viral Engineering considered
expectations.evidence: No mention of 'memetic', 'viral', 'meme', or 'viral engineering' appears anywhere in the transcript or output files. No candidates are evaluated for viral/memetic potential. The shortlist evaluation criteria do not include shareability or memetic qualities. The techniques_used list in 01-candidates.md does not include memetic or viral engineering.

claims.evidence: Counting numbered entries in 01-candidates.md: #1-118 in generation, #119-184 in riffing = 184 total numbered entries. Deduplication log shows 4 duplicates removed, consistent with 190 pre-dedup / 184 post-dedup count. The 01-candidates.md frontmatter says 190 total_unique_candidates, which may count some unlisted extras or be slightly inflated, but the core count of 184 numbered entries is verified.

The transcript lists 11 categories with candidates generated for each. However, the SKILL.md standard 11 categories and the 11 listed in the transcript differ somewhat — the transcript substitutes 'Dark Nature', 'Cultural Tension', and 'Subculture Code-Switch' for standard categories like 'Descriptive', 'Acronymic', and 'Metaphorical'. The frontmatter lists categories_not_attempted: [descriptive, acronymic, institutional_compound, metaphorical], which is 4 skipped standard categories. So 11 categories were attempted but they are not the canonical 11 — rather 7 standard + 4 consumer/brief-specific substitutions.

01-candidates.md frontmatter lists 28 items in techniques_used array. Cross-referencing: the riffing section demonstrates synonym_explosion, metaphor_shift, outcome_shift, sensory_shift, root_compression, language_shift, structural_shift, adjacent_metaphor, conceptual_blending, alternate_real_spellings, contrarian — all labeled inline. Generation demonstrates five_senses_scan, found_word_generation, etc. Some techniques like desired_self_naming and color_name_mapping are claimed but not demonstrated with labeled candidates.

claims.claim: All 15 names passed tag test, badgeability, social-native
claims.evidence: The claim is asserted in aggregate in the consumer riffing checklist but no per-name evaluation is shown. We cannot verify whether each of the 15 names actually passed these tests — the results are stated as a blanket pass. Names like 'Stave' or 'Dross' could arguably struggle on some tests but no discussion is provided.

claims.claim: Riffs per seed: Offcut: 15, Soot: 12, Feral: 12, Briar: 11, Spite: 11, Moth: 11
claims.evidence: Counting actual listed riffs: Offcut has 13 (#119-131), Soot has 11 (#132-142), Feral has 11 (#143-153), Briar has 9 (#154-162), Spite has 11 (#163-173), Moth has 11 (#174-184). The frontmatter claims are inflated for Offcut (claims 15, has 13), Soot (claims 12, has 11), Feral (claims 12, has 11), and Briar (claims 11, has 9). Total listed is 66, matching the transcript claim, but per-seed counts are inconsistent with the listed candidates.

suggestions.assertion: Arbitrary/Absurd Association heavily weighted (per SKILL.md fashion guidance)
suggestions.reason: What counts as 'heavily weighted' is subjective. 10 of 118 generation candidates (8.5%) is healthy but not dominant. The assertion would be stronger if it specified a minimum count or percentage, e.g., 'at least 10 arbitrary/absurd candidates generated' or 'arbitrary/absurd in top-3 categories by volume.'

suggestions.assertion: Badgeability/Wearability test applied (would you wear this on a hat?)
suggestions.reason: The test is mentioned in criteria and claimed as passed, but no per-name evidence is shown. A stronger assertion would be 'badgeability test results shown per shortlisted name' which would catch blanket claims vs. actual evaluation.

This failed cleanly, but it may be a niche expectation for a streetwear brief. If the skill doesn't include memetic/viral engineering as a standard technique, this assertion tests something the skill may not be designed to do. Consider whether this is truly a skill expectation or a nice-to-have.

No assertion checks the quality or diversity of the shortlist — e.g., whether shortlisted names span multiple territories rather than clustering in one, or whether the shortlist includes at least one name from each major technique category. The actual shortlist is heavily Found Word (12 of 15), which may indicate insufficient diversity despite broad generation.

No assertion verifies that the consumer-naming.md reference was actually loaded during execution. The transcript claims it was loaded but this is self-reported. A stronger check would look for consumer-specific technique outputs (e.g., Japanese sound-symbolism candidates or sensory dissonance names) that could only come from that reference.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are well-targeted for this consumer/fashion brief. The three failures (Desired-Self Naming, Trend-Cycle Audit, Memetic/Viral Engineering) reflect genuine gaps in the execution — techniques that were either not applied or not demonstrated. The strongest assertions are those checking for specific technique outputs (Five Senses Scan, Subculture Code-Switch) rather than metadata claims. Consider tightening assertions that can be satisfied by metadata alone.
